title: Linux Server Monitoring and Maintenance Essentials
description: Learn to monitor system resources, manage logs, configure automatic security updates, and set up basic alerting with cron jobs on your Ubuntu server.
prerequisites: A running Ubuntu 24.04 server with sudo access
Basic familiarity with the command line
learningObjectives: Monitor CPU, memory, and disk usage with standard Linux tools
Use journalctl to read and filter systemd logs
Configure logrotate to manage log file sizes
Set up automatic security updates with unattended-upgrades
Write a simple monitoring script and schedule it with cron

sponsorCta.text: You will need a Linux server for this exercise. DigitalOcean makes it easy to spin one up in under a minute.

steps.title: Monitor System Resources
steps.description: Get a quick overview of your server's health using built-in tools. These commands are the first thing you run when something feels slow or a service stops responding.
steps.expectedOutput:
14:23:07 up 3 days,  2:15,  1 user,  load average: 0.08, 0.03, 0.01

               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           976Mi       210Mi       120Mi       1.0Mi       645Mi       610Mi
Swap:            0B          0B          0B

Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/vda1        25G  4.2G   20G  18% /
tmpfs           489M     0  489M   0% /dev/shm

[htop opens an interactive process viewer - press q to exit]
hints: The load average in uptime shows system load over 1, 5, and 15 minutes
Values in 'available' under free -h are more useful than 'free' because Linux uses free RAM for disk cache
If df shows a filesystem over 80% used, plan to clean up or expand the disk
In htop, press F6 to sort by different columns (CPU%, MEM%, etc.)
validationCriteria: You can read the load average from uptime output
You understand the difference between 'free' and 'available' memory
You can identify disk usage percentages
You can launch and navigate htop

steps.title: Monitor Processes and Network Connections
steps.description: Identify what processes are using the most resources and which network ports are in use.
steps.expectedOutput:
USER         PID %CPU %MEM    VSZ   RSS TTY      STAT START   TIME COMMAND
root           1  0.0  1.1 168344 11200 ?        Ss   Mar23   0:05 /lib/systemd/systemd
mysql        845  0.1  8.2 1234567 82340 ?       Ssl  Mar23   1:23 /usr/sbin/mysqld
www-data     902  0.0  0.5  55432  5120 ?        S    Mar23   0:00 nginx: worker process
...

State    Recv-Q   Send-Q   Local Address:Port   Peer Address:Port   Process
LISTEN   0        511      0.0.0.0:80           0.0.0.0:*           users:(("nginx",pid=901,fd=6))
LISTEN   0        511      0.0.0.0:443          0.0.0.0:*           users:(("nginx",pid=901,fd=7))
LISTEN   0        128      0.0.0.0:22           0.0.0.0:*           users:(("sshd",pid=700,fd=3))

Total: 45
TCP:   8 (estab 2, closed 1, orphaned 0, timewait 0)
...
hints: ps aux --sort=-%mem sorts processes by memory usage, highest first
ss replaced the older netstat command; it is faster and shows more detail
The -t flag filters for TCP connections, -l for listening, -n for numeric ports, -p for process names
If you see unexpected ports listening, investigate with: sudo lsof -i :PORT
validationCriteria: You can identify the top memory and CPU consumers
You can list all listening ports and their associated processes
You can read the ss connection summary

steps.title: Read and Filter Logs with journalctl
steps.description: systemd's journal collects logs from all services. journalctl is how you read them. Learning to filter effectively saves a lot of time when debugging.
steps.expectedOutput:
-- Journal begins at ... --
Mar 26 14:00:01 ubuntu-lab CRON[12345]: ...
Mar 26 14:01:23 ubuntu-lab nginx[901]: ...
...

-- No entries --
(or relevant SSH log entries)

Mar 26 12:00:00 ubuntu-lab kernel: ...

Archived and active journals take up 48.0M in the file system.
hints: -n 20 shows the last 20 log entries
-u filters by systemd unit (service name)
--since and --until accept human-readable dates like 'yesterday', '2 hours ago', '2026-03-25'
-p err shows only error-level messages and above (crit, alert, emerg)
Add -f to follow logs in real time, like tail -f
validationCriteria: You can view recent journal entries
You can filter logs by service and time range
You can find error-level messages
You know the journal's disk usage

steps.title: Configure Log Rotation
steps.description: Log files grow over time and can fill your disk. logrotate compresses and rotates old logs automatically. Nginx installs its own logrotate config, but you should understand how to create one for custom applications.
commands:
sudo tee /etc/logrotate.d/myapp << 'EOF'
/var/log/myapp/*.log {
    daily
    missingok
    rotate 14
    compress
    delaycompress
    notifempty
    create 0640 www-data www-data
    sharedscripts
    postrotate
        systemctl reload nginx > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
    endscript
}
EOF
steps.expectedOutput:
# Nginx's default logrotate config will be displayed

# Your custom config is created

reading config file /etc/logrotate.d/myapp
...
rotating pattern: /var/log/myapp/*.log  after 1 days (14 rotations)
empty log files are not rotated, old logs are removed
...
hints: The -d flag runs logrotate in debug mode without actually rotating anything
daily means rotate once per day; you can also use weekly or monthly
rotate 14 keeps the last 14 rotated files before deleting them
compress uses gzip; delaycompress skips compression on the most recent rotated file
missingok prevents errors if the log file does not exist yet
validationCriteria: You understand the Nginx logrotate config
Your custom logrotate config is valid (no errors in debug mode)
The rotation settings match your requirements

steps.title: Set Up Automatic Security Updates
steps.description: Keeping your server patched is critical. Ubuntu's unattended-upgrades package can automatically install security updates so you do not have to do it manually every day.
steps.expectedOutput:
The following NEW packages will be installed:
  unattended-upgrades
...

(dpkg-reconfigure shows a dialog - select Yes)

APT::Periodic::Update-Package-Lists "1";
APT::Periodic::Unattended-Upgrade "1";

Starting unattended upgrades script
...
No packages found that can be upgraded unattended and target reboot_required
Unattended upgrade returned: True
hints: Select 'Yes' when dpkg-reconfigure asks to enable automatic updates
The '1' in 20auto-upgrades means run daily; '0' would disable it
By default, only security updates are installed automatically
Check /var/log/unattended-upgrades/ for logs of what was updated
The dry run shows what would be updated without actually installing anything
validationCriteria: unattended-upgrades is installed
Automatic updates are enabled in /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/20auto-upgrades
The dry run completes without errors

steps.title: Create a Simple Monitoring Script
steps.description: Write a shell script that checks disk usage, memory, and load average. If any value crosses a threshold, it writes a warning to a log file. You can later extend this to send email or Slack notifications.
commands:
sudo tee /opt/scripts/server-health.sh << 'SCRIPT'
#!/bin/bash

LOG_FILE="/var/log/server-health.log"
DISK_THRESHOLD=80
MEM_THRESHOLD=90
LOAD_THRESHOLD=$(nproc)

timestamp=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')

# Check disk usage
disk_usage=$(df / | awk 'NR==2 {print $5}' | tr -d '%')
if [ "$disk_usage" -gt "$DISK_THRESHOLD" ]; then
    echo "[$timestamp] WARNING: Disk usage is ${disk_usage}% (threshold: ${DISK_THRESHOLD}%)" >> "$LOG_FILE"
fi

# Check memory usage
mem_usage=$(free | awk '/Mem:/ {printf "%.0f", $3/$2 * 100}')
if [ "$mem_usage" -gt "$MEM_THRESHOLD" ]; then
    echo "[$timestamp] WARNING: Memory usage is ${mem_usage}% (threshold: ${MEM_THRESHOLD}%)" >> "$LOG_FILE"
fi

# Check load average (1 minute)
load_avg=$(awk '{print $1}' /proc/loadavg)
overloaded=$(echo "$load_avg > $LOAD_THRESHOLD" | bc -l)
if [ "$overloaded" -eq 1 ]; then
    echo "[$timestamp] WARNING: Load average is ${load_avg} (threshold: ${LOAD_THRESHOLD})" >> "$LOG_FILE"
fi

# Always log a health check entry
echo "[$timestamp] Health check: disk=${disk_usage}% mem=${mem_usage}% load=${load_avg}" >> "$LOG_FILE"
SCRIPT
hints: The LOAD_THRESHOLD is set to the number of CPU cores, which is a reasonable default
You can test threshold alerts by temporarily lowering the values in the script
bc -l is needed for floating-point comparison (load average is a decimal)
To add email alerts, you can install mailutils and use the mail command
validationCriteria: The script runs without errors
A health check entry appears in /var/log/server-health.log
Threshold comparisons work correctly

steps.title: Schedule the Monitoring Script with Cron
steps.description: Use cron to run the health check script every 5 minutes. Cron is the standard job scheduler on Linux and is useful for any recurring task.
commands: sudo crontab -l 2>/dev/null; echo 'Current crontab (may be empty)'

echo '*/5 * * * * /opt/scripts/server-health.sh' | sudo tee -a /var/spool/cron/crontabs/root > /dev/null || (sudo crontab -l 2>/dev/null; echo '*/5 * * * * /opt/scripts/server-health.sh') | sudo crontab -
# Wait a few minutes, then check the log
steps.expectedOutput:
*/5 * * * * /opt/scripts/server-health.sh

[2026-03-26 14:30:00] Health check: disk=18% mem=22% load=0.05
[2026-03-26 14:35:00] Health check: disk=18% mem=21% load=0.02
hints: */5 * * * * means every 5 minutes
The five fields in a cron expression are: minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week
Use crontab -e to edit the crontab in a text editor if you prefer
cron runs as root when you use sudo crontab, so the script has full access
To remove the job later: sudo crontab -e and delete the line
validationCriteria: The cron job is listed in root's crontab
New entries appear in server-health.log every 5 minutes
The script runs without errors under cron

completionCriteria: You can check CPU, memory, and disk usage with standard tools
You can filter systemd journal logs by service, time, and severity
A custom logrotate config is in place
A monitoring script runs on a cron schedule and logs health data

troubleshooting.issue: Cron job does not seem to run
troubleshooting.solution: Check that the script is executable (chmod +x). Verify the cron job is listed with 'sudo crontab -l'. Look at /var/log/syslog for cron-related errors: sudo grep CRON /var/log/syslog.
Run 'sudo apt update' first to refresh the package list. If you see lock file errors, wait for any running apt process to finish, or check with 'ps aux | grep apt'.

troubleshooting.issue: journalctl shows 'No journal files were found'
troubleshooting.solution: The journal storage may not be persistent. Run 'sudo mkdir -p /var/log/journal && sudo systemctl restart systemd-journald' to enable persistent storage.
